filename: Public Lands Institute - Elephant Rocks State Park - 001.tif
description:
Granite boulders and bare winter trees at Elephant Rocks State Park, St. Francois Mountains, Missouri. The boulders are 1.47-billion-year-old Graniteville Granite (Proterozoic, Rhyacian period), shaped by spheroidal weathering along orthogonal joint sets. Among the oldest exposed rock in the midcontinental United States. Osage (Ni-U-Ko'n-Ska). The territory was ceded to the United States through the 1808 Treaty with the Osage, which transferred Osage land claims east of the Osage River in present-day Missouri; the 1825 Treaty of St. Louis extinguished remaining Osage interests in the state.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/elephant-rocks/
categories: Black and white photographs of Missouri

filename: Public Lands Institute - Elephant Rocks State Park - 002.tif
description:
Spheroidally weathered granite boulders at Elephant Rocks State Park, St. Francois Mountains, Missouri. 1.47-billion-year-old Graniteville Granite (Proterozoic, Rhyacian period) with characteristic rounding produced by chemical weathering. Osage (Ni-U-Ko'n-Ska). The territory was ceded to the United States through the 1808 Treaty with the Osage, which transferred Osage land claims east of the Osage River in present-day Missouri; the 1825 Treaty of St. Louis extinguished remaining Osage interests in the state. Lichen communities visible on granite surfaces.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/elephant-rocks/
categories: Black and white photographs of Missouri

filename: Public Lands Institute - Elephant Rocks State Park - 003.tif
description:
Massive granite boulders at Elephant Rocks State Park, St. Francois Mountains, Missouri. 1.47-billion-year-old Graniteville Granite (Proterozoic, Rhyacian period). The rounded forms result from spheroidal weathering of the pluton along joint planes over hundreds of millions of years. Osage (Ni-U-Ko'n-Ska). The territory was ceded to the United States through the 1808 Treaty with the Osage, which transferred Osage land claims east of the Osage River in present-day Missouri; the 1825 Treaty of St. Louis extinguished remaining Osage interests in the state.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/elephant-rocks/
categories: Black and white photographs of Missouri

filename: Public Lands Institute - Elephant Rocks State Park - 004.tif
description:
Spheroidal weathering in 1.47-billion-year-old Graniteville Granite at Elephant Rocks State Park, St. Francois Mountains, Missouri. The massive boulder demonstrates characteristic rounding produced by chemical weathering along orthogonal joint sets in the Proterozoic (Rhyacian period) pluton. The bedrock slab in the foreground preserves historic visitor inscriptions. Osage (Ni-U-Ko'n-Ska). The territory was ceded to the United States through the 1808 Treaty with the Osage, which transferred Osage land claims east of the Osage River in present-day Missouri; the 1825 Treaty of St. Louis extinguished remaining Osage interests in the state.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/elephant-rocks/
categories: Black and white photographs of Missouri

filename: Public Lands Institute - Johnson's Shut-Ins State Park - 001.tif
description:
East Fork Black River and Proterozoic rhyolite formations at Johnson's Shut-Ins State Park, St. Francois Mountains, Missouri. The shut-ins are formed where the river is confined to narrow channels carved into 1.47-billion-year-old igneous bedrock, among the oldest exposed rock in Missouri. Osage (Ni-U-Ko'n-Ska). The territory was ceded to the United States through the 1808 Treaty with the Osage, which transferred Osage land claims east of the Osage River in present-day Missouri; the 1825 Treaty of St. Louis extinguished remaining Osage interests in the state.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/johnsons-shut-ins/
categories: Black and white photographs of Missouri

filename: Public Lands Institute - Palo Duro Canyon State Park - 001.tif
description:
Palo Duro Canyon, the second-largest canyon in the United States, at Palo Duro Canyon State Park, Texas. Exposed Triassic strata including the Tecovas Formation (~225 Mya) and Trujillo Formation (~218 Mya). Canyon incision began during the Pliocene (~5 Mya) along the Prairie Dog Town Fork of the Red River. Comanche (Numunu), Kiowa. The canyon was a primary Comanche and Kiowa encampment site until September 1874, when Colonel Ranald Mackenzie's forces destroyed the camps at the Battle of Palo Duro Canyon during the Red River War. The surviving bands were subsequently confined to the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache Reservation in present-day Oklahoma, established under the 1867 Medicine Lodge Treaty.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/palo-duro-canyon/
categories: Black and white photographs of Texas

filename: Public Lands Institute - Palo Duro Canyon State Park - 002.tif
description:
Triassic canyon wall formations at Palo Duro Canyon State Park, Texas. Exposed Tecovas Formation and Trujillo Formation strata (252 to 201 million years old). Palo Duro Canyon is the second-largest canyon in the United States. Comanche (Numunu), Kiowa. The canyon was a primary Comanche and Kiowa encampment site until September 1874, when Colonel Ranald Mackenzie's forces destroyed the camps at the Battle of Palo Duro Canyon during the Red River War. The surviving bands were subsequently confined to the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache Reservation in present-day Oklahoma, established under the 1867 Medicine Lodge Treaty.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/palo-duro-canyon/
categories: Black and white photographs of Texas

Eroded Triassic sedimentary formations at Palo Duro Canyon State Park, Texas. The canyon exposes the Tecovas Formation (~225 Mya) and Trujillo Formation (~218 Mya) along the Prairie Dog Town Fork of the Red River. Comanche (Numunu), Kiowa. The canyon was a primary Comanche and Kiowa encampment site until September 1874, when Colonel Ranald Mackenzie's forces destroyed the camps at the Battle of Palo Duro Canyon during the Red River War. The surviving bands were subsequently confined to the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache Reservation in present-day Oklahoma, established under the 1867 Medicine Lodge Treaty.

description:
Canyon landscape at Palo Duro Canyon State Park, Texas. Triassic-age strata (Tecovas and Trujillo formations) with juniper-mesquite canyon scrub in the Chihuahuan Desert transition zone. Second-largest canyon in the United States. Comanche (Numunu), Kiowa. The canyon was a primary Comanche and Kiowa encampment site until September 1874, when Colonel Ranald Mackenzie's forces destroyed the camps at the Battle of Palo Duro Canyon during the Red River War. The surviving bands were subsequently confined to the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache Reservation in present-day Oklahoma, established under the 1867 Medicine Lodge Treaty.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/palo-duro-canyon/
categories: Black and white photographs of Texas

filename: Public Lands Institute - Palo Duro Canyon State Park - 005.tif

Canyon incision began during the Pliocene (~5 Mya). Comanche (Numunu), Kiowa. The canyon was a primary Comanche and Kiowa encampment site until September 1874, when Colonel Ranald Mackenzie's forces destroyed the camps at the Battle of Palo Duro Canyon during the Red River War. The surviving bands were subsequently confined to the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache Reservation in present-day Oklahoma, established under the 1867 Medicine Lodge Treaty.

categories: Black and white photographs of Texas

filename: Public Lands Institute - Caprock Canyon State Park - 011.tif
description:
Caprock escarpment landscape at Caprock Canyon State Park, Texas. Permian Quartermaster Formation (~280 Mya) overlain by Pliocene caliche caprock (~5 Mya). Comanche (Numunu), Kiowa. The Llano Estacado and surrounding caprock escarpment were Comanche and Kiowa territory until the Red River War of 1874, after which the surviving bands were confined to the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache Reservation in present-day Oklahoma; the land was subsequently opened to ranching and eventually transferred to State of Texas ownership. Home to the official Texas State Bison Herd, the last free-ranging southern plains bison lineage.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/caprock-canyon/
categories: Black and white photographs of Texas

filename: Public Lands Institute - Charon's Garden Wilderness Area - 001.tif
description:
Cambrian granite outcrop and mixed-grass prairie at Charon's Garden Wilderness Area, Wichita Mountains, Oklahoma. ~525-million-year-old bedrock (Furongian epoch), among the oldest exposed rock in the southern Great Plains. Federal wilderness area within Wichita Mountains National Wildlife Refuge; no mechanized access. Wichita (Kitikiti'sh), Comanche, Kiowa. The area fell within the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache Reservation established under the 1867 Medicine Lodge Treaty; following allotment under the Jerome Agreement (ratified 1900), surplus lands were opened to non-Indian settlement and the federal government designated the surrounding area as a forest and game reserve in 1901.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/charons-garden/
categories: Black and white photographs of Oklahoma

filename: Public Lands Institute - Charon's Garden Wilderness Area - 002.tif
description:
Cambrian granite boulder field at Charon's Garden Wilderness Area, Wichita Mountains, Oklahoma. 525-million-year-old basement rock exhumed through erosion of overlying Paleozoic and Mesozoic cover. Wichita (Kitikiti'sh), Comanche, Kiowa. The area fell within the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache Reservation established under the 1867 Medicine Lodge Treaty; following allotment under the Jerome Agreement (ratified 1900), surplus lands were opened to non-Indian settlement and the federal government designated the surrounding area as a forest and game reserve in 1901. Post oak savanna and granite outcrop community.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/charons-garden/
categories: Black and white photographs of Oklahoma

filename: Public Lands Institute - Charon's Garden Wilderness Area - 003.tif
description:
Mixed-grass prairie and Cambrian granite at Charon's Garden Wilderness Area, Wichita Mountains, Oklahoma. ~525-million-year-old igneous bedrock with ephemeral stream drainage and vernal pools in boulder depressions. Federally designated wilderness area. Wichita (Kitikiti'sh), Comanche, Kiowa. The area fell within the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache Reservation established under the 1867 Medicine Lodge Treaty; following allotment under the Jerome Agreement (ratified 1900), surplus lands were opened to non-Indian settlement and the federal government designated the surrounding area as a forest and game reserve in 1901.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/charons-garden/
categories: Black and white photographs of Oklahoma

filename: Public Lands Institute - French Park - 001.tif
description:
Mixed mesophytic forest at French Park, Hamilton County, Ohio. Ordovician limestone and shale bedrock (~450 Mya, Cincinnatian Series). Miami (Myaamia), Shawnee. The territory was ceded to the United States through the 1795 Treaty of Greenville following the Battle of Fallen Timbers. Remaining Miami and Shawnee populations were subsequently displaced through a series of treaties and forced removal west of the Mississippi by the 1840s. Oak-hickory woodland with beech-maple slopes and pawpaw thickets along Amberley Creek.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/french-park/
categories: Black and white photographs of Ohio

filename: Public Lands Institute - French Park - 002.tif
description:
Winter woodland at French Park, Hamilton County, Ohio. Amberley Creek corridor with exposed Ordovician limestone and shale (~450 Mya, Cincinnatian Series). Miami (Myaamia), Shawnee. The territory was ceded to the United States through the 1795 Treaty of Greenville following the Battle of Fallen Timbers. Remaining Miami and Shawnee populations were subsequently displaced through a series of treaties and forced removal west of the Mississippi by the 1840s. The creek is a tributary of Mill Creek, draining to the Ohio River.
Source: Public Lands Institute -- publiclandsinstitute.net/sites/french-park/
categories: Black and white photographs of Ohio
